1: ʾê zeh sēfer kĕrîtût ʾimkĕkem — "where is the certificate of divorce of your mother?" — the rhetorical question assumes no such document exists. sēfer kĕrîtût (H3748) is the technical legal term for a writ of divorce (cf. Deuteronomy 24:1,3; Jeremiah 3:8). YHWH challenges Israel to produce evidence of formal divorce — and cannot be produced. Parallel question: ûmî minnōšay ʾăšer mākartî etkem lô — "to which of my creditors did I sell you?" — the second legal scenario (debt-slavery) is also dismissed. hēn baʿăwōnōtêkem nimkartem — "for your iniquities you were sold" — the exile was self-caused through sin, not divine abandonment.
2: maddûaʿ bāʾtî wĕʾên ʾîš — "why was there no one when I came?" — the lament that YHWH's arrival for rescue met no response. qārāʾtî wĕʾên ʿōneh — "I called and there was no one who answered" — the failure of responsiveness mirrors the earlier covenant accusation (cf. Isaiah 65:12; Isaiah 66:4). Then the counter-challenge: hăqāṣōr qāṣĕrâ yādî mippĕdût — "is my hand too short to ransom?" (pĕdût, H6304, redemption/ransom). The rhetorical question expects "no" — power to save exists; the problem is Israel's unresponsiveness.
3: ʾalbîš šāmayim qadrût wĕśaq ʾāśîm kĕsûtām — "I clothe the heavens in blackness and make sackcloth their covering" — YHWH can reverse creation's light. The cosmic darkness imagery inverts the creation of light (Genesis 1:3) and anticipates the darkness at the crucifixion (Matthew 27:45). The śaq (sackcloth) of the heavens mirrors the human mourning garment — heaven mourns with the same clothing worn at funerals. YHWH's demonstration of power establishes that the failure of salvation is not YHWH's incapacity but human refusal.
4: ʾădōnāy Yhwh nātan lî lĕšôn limmudîm — "the Lord YHWH has given me the tongue of those who are taught/disciples" (limmudîm, H3929, the taught ones — plural of limmûd, from lāmaḏ, to teach/learn). The Servant is specifically characterized as a learner who speaks from instruction received, not from innate authority. lāḏaʿat lāʿût ʾet-yaʿēp dābār — "to know how to sustain with a word him who is weary" — the purpose of the instructed tongue is pastoral: to support the exhausted with speech. yaʿîr lî ʾōzen babbōqer babbōqer — "he wakens my ear morning by morning" — the daily spiritual formation; the repetition of babbōqer (morning) emphasizes the continuity of divine instruction.
5: ʾădōnāy Yhwh pātaḥ lî ʾōzen wĕʾānōkî lōʾ mārîtî — "the Lord YHWH opened my ear, and I was not rebellious" (mārar, H4784, to be rebellious). The opening of the ear by YHWH is the prerequisite for obedience — divine action precedes human response. The Servant's obedience is explicitly contrasted with Israel's rebellion in Isaiah 1-39. ʾāḥôr lōʾ nasûgtî — "I did not turn backward" — the refusal to retreat under pressure. The steadfast obedience is a character description, not merely a single act.
6: gēwî nātattî lammakkîm ûlĕḥāyay lĕmōrĕṭîm — "I gave my back to those who strike (nākâ), and my cheeks to those who pluck out the beard." The beard-plucking (māraṭ, H4803, to pluck/pull smooth) was a profound degradation in ANE culture — it was done to captive enemies and was considered deeply shameful. pānay lōʾ hissatirtî mikelimmôt wārōq — "I did not hide my face from disgrace and spitting" (rōq, H7536, spittle). The three anatomical elements — back, cheeks, face — span the full range of body-oriented humiliation. Matthew 26:67; Mark 14:65; John 19:1 each fulfill one or more of these Servant-song elements at the Passion.
7: wĕʾădōnāy Yhwh yaʿăzor lî ʿal-kēn lōʾ niklāmtî — "but the Lord YHWH helps me; therefore I was not disgraced" — the divine assistance transforms the suffering from defeat into endurance. ʿal-kēn śamtî pānay kĕḥallāmîš — "therefore I set my face like flint" (ḥallāmîš, H2496, flint — the hardest stone, used for cutting tools). The flint-face is a metaphor for absolute determination in the face of opposition. Luke 9:51 — "he set his face to go to Jerusalem" (estēriksen to prosōpon autou) — echoes this Servant posture in Jesus's commitment to the Passion journey. wĕʾēdaʿ kî-lōʾ ʾēbôš — "and I know I will not be put to shame."
8: qārôb maṣdîqî — "near is the one who vindicates me" (ṣādaq in the hiphil, to declare righteous/acquit). The Servant shifts into forensic (courtroom) language — YHWH is his vindicator against accusers. mî yārîḇ ʾittî naʿămdâ yāḥad — "who will contend with me? let us stand up together." The legal challenge is issued to any would-be accuser. mî-baʿal mišpāṭî yiggaš ʾēlay — "who is the master of my lawsuit? let him approach me." Paul uses this forensic logic in Romans 8:33-34: "Who shall bring any charge against God's elect? It is God who justifies. Who is to condemn?" — the Servant's vindication-by-YHWH becomes the basis for the believer's freedom from condemnation in Christ.
9: hēn ʾădōnāy Yhwh yaʿăzor lî mî hûʾ yarširʿēnî — "behold, the Lord YHWH helps me; who will declare me guilty?" — the divine vindication makes human condemnation futile. hēn kullām kabbegaḏ yiblû — "behold, all of them will wear out like a garment" — the accusers/opponents will decay while the Servant endures. ʿāš yōʾklēm — "the moth will eat them" — the same moth-eaten garment imagery as Isaiah 51:8 (where YHWH's righteousness endures while the nations rot like moth-eaten wool). The language anticipates Hebrews 1:11 (the heavens wearing out like a garment) contrasted with Christ's permanence.
10: mî bākem yĕrēʾ Yhwh šōmēaʿ bĕqôl ʿabdô — "who among you fears YHWH and obeys the voice of his servant?" — the Servant's instruction is now mediated to the community. The one who walks in darkness (ʾăšer hālak ḥăšēkîm wĕʾên nōgah lô, "who walks in darkness and has no light") is called to trust (yiḇṭaḥ) in YHWH's name and rely (yiššāʿēn) on his God. The model of trust-in-darkness echoes Habakkuk 2:4 ("the righteous will live by his faithfulness") and anticipates the NT call to walk by faith, not by sight (2 Corinthians 5:7).
11: hēn kullĕkem qōdĕḥê ʾēš mĕʾazzĕrê zîqôt — "behold, all of you who kindle fire and equip yourselves with burning torches" — those who manufacture their own light as a substitute for trusting in YHWH. lĕkû bĕʾûr ʾeškĕkem — "walk in the light of your fire" — the ironic permission to continue in self-made illumination. miyyādî hāyĕtâ-zōʾt lākem lĕmaʿăṣēbâ tišhābûn — "this you have from my hand: you will lie down in torment" (maʿăṣēbâ, H4620, pain/torment). The self-made fire — human wisdom, political maneuvering, idolatry — becomes the source of final torment. The chapter closes with the judgment oracle against self-reliance as the anti-type to the Servant's trust in YHWH (Isaiah 50:4-9).
